blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Keeping Score: Foxes, Hedgehogs, and the Tournament
The ideas in 30 seconds
Most expert forecasts are unjudgeable by design: without explicit probabilities, timelines, and defined terms, any outcome can be spun as a win.
The Brier score makes accuracy measurable by combining calibration (do your 70% calls come true 70% of the time?) with resolution (the courage to move away from a safe 50%).
Tetlock's twenty-year EPJ study — 284 experts, roughly 28,000 predictions — found that how experts think beats what they know: multi-perspective "foxes" outforecast one-big-idea "hedgehogs."
Fame and accuracy were inversely correlated: the more media attention an expert received, the worse their forecasts, because television rewards confident hedgehog stories.

In the IARPA forecasting tournament launched after the Iraq WMD fiasco, the Good Judgment Project's volunteers beat every rival team and reportedly outperformed intelligence analysts with access to classified information.
Superforecaster status is skill, not luck: about 70% of the top 2% stayed in the top ranks the following year, with far less regression to the mean than chance would produce.
Vague Forecasts Are Designed to Be Unjudgeable

To score a forecast, you first need a forecast that can be scored. Most punditry fails this test: predictions come without deadlines ("China's rise will falter"), without probabilities ("a recession is possible"), and with elastic terms ("may," "could," "significant") that fit any outcome. After the fact, the forecaster grades their own work — and, thanks to hindsight and motivated reasoning, almost always passes.

The classic demonstration comes from Sherman Kent, the father of modern intelligence analysis. A 1951 National Intelligence Estimate judged a Soviet attack on Yugoslavia a "serious possibility." When Kent asked the analysts who had signed off on that phrase what it meant, their answers ranged from roughly 20% to 80% — the same words, in the same document, meant opposite things to the people who wrote them.

Kent proposed a fix: a standard schedule mapping words to numbers (e.g., "almost certain" meaning about 93%, "probable" about 75%). Colleagues rejected it as unseemly, sneering that numbers made analysts sound like bookies. Kent's retort was that he'd rather be a bookie than a poet — but the culture of comfortable vagueness won, and it still dominates punditry today.

The lesson: a judgeable forecast requires (1) a precisely defined event, (2) an explicit time frame, and (3) a numeric probability. Anything less is not a prediction; it's insurance against ever being wrong. A subtlety follows: a single probabilistic forecast can never be proven wrong by a single outcome — a 70% call that fails might just be the 30% happening. Judging skill therefore requires many forecasts, which is exactly why forecasting had to become a numbers game.
Brier Scores: Calibration Plus Resolution

Once forecasts are numeric, accuracy can be computed. The standard tool is the Brier score, devised by Glenn Brier in 1950: the squared difference between the probabilities you assigned and what actually happened, summed across forecasts. Zero is omniscient perfection. On yes/no questions, 0.5 is what mindless 50/50 guessing earns, and 2.0 is perfect wrongness — saying 100% for things that never happen.

The score decomposes into two virtues. Calibration means your probabilities match reality's frequencies: of all the events you called 60% likely, about 60% occurred. Resolution means decisiveness: you assign high probabilities to things that happen and low ones to things that don't, rather than huddling at "maybe." A perpetual fence-sitter can be perfectly calibrated yet useless; a bold forecaster who's usually right scores far better. Good forecasting demands both.

Raw scores also need context. A weathercaster in Phoenix can score near zero by predicting sun every day; the same score in changeable Springfield would signal genius. So Brier scores only mean something against benchmarks: chance, simple extrapolation rules ("predict no change"), and other forecasters answering the same questions over the same period. That last requirement — same questions, same time frame, head-to-head — is what a forecasting tournament provides.
EPJ: Foxes Beat Hedgehogs, and Fame Predicts Failure

Tetlock built exactly that. His Expert Political Judgment (EPJ) study, begun in 1984 and published in 2005, recruited 284 experts — academics, journalists, intelligence and government officials — and collected roughly 28,000 predictions about political and economic events, all with defined outcomes, deadlines, and probabilities.

The headline (covered in chapter 1) was that the average expert roughly matched a dart-throwing chimp on long-range questions. The deeper finding was that the average concealed two distinct groups, separated not by credentials, ideology, or access to secrets, but by style of thinking. Borrowing Isaiah Berlin's essay, Tetlock called them hedgehogs and foxes.

Hedgehogs know One Big Thing — a governing idea (Marxism, libertarianism, environmental doom, whatever) — and stretch it to explain everything. They pile up reasons they're right, radiate confidence, and love the words "moreover" and "certain." Their accuracy was dismal, dipping below chance at longer horizons, and — most damning — they got worse inside their own specialty, where the Big Idea gripped hardest.

Foxes know many little things. They aggregate multiple sources and perspectives, talk in "however" and "on the other hand," treat beliefs as hypotheses, and accept ambiguity. Foxes beat chance, beat hedgehogs, and were far better calibrated — genuine, if modest, foresight, especially at shorter horizons.

Then the perverse twist: fame correlated inversely with accuracy. The more often an expert appeared in the media, the worse their predictions. The explanation is supply and demand: audiences want simple, confident stories, and hedgehogs deliver them. The qualities that make a great talking head — certainty, a single clean narrative — are precisely the qualities that ruin forecasts. The market for expertise rewards the wrong thing because nobody keeps score.
After Iraq's Missing WMD: The IARPA Tournament

In 2002 the US intelligence community — 20,000-odd analysts across multiple agencies, with a budget north of $50 billion a year — judged it a near-certainty that Saddam Hussein had weapons of mass destruction. He didn't. The postmortem commissions found the IC hadn't seriously tested alternative hypotheses and had expressed certainty the evidence couldn't support. Tetlock's key point: the sin wasn't being wrong — probabilistic forecasts are sometimes wrong — it was claiming ~100% when an honest process might have said 60-70%, a difference that could have changed the war debate.

One institutional response was IARPA (Intelligence Advanced Research Projects Activity), a research agency modeled on DARPA. In 2011 it launched the ACE tournament (Aggregative Contingent Estimation): five university-led teams competed to forecast roughly 500 real geopolitical questions over four years — will Greece leave the eurozone, will North Korea test a nuclear device by a given date — with daily-updatable probabilities scored by Brier against identical questions and deadlines.

Tetlock, Barbara Mellers, and Don Moore entered the Good Judgment Project (GJP): thousands of ordinary volunteers — retirees, programmers, homemakers — recruited online and randomized into experimental conditions (training, teaming, tracking of top performers). The results were lopsided. GJP beat the official control group by 60% in year one and 78% in year two, and outperformed the other academic teams by 30% to 70% — so decisively that IARPA dropped the rivals after two years. By press accounts, GJP's best even beat professional intelligence analysts who had access to classified information by roughly 30% on the same questions. Amateurs with Google, kept honest by scoring, out-forecast the professionals.
Superforecasters: Skill, Not Luck

GJP's top 2% each year were dubbed superforecasters — people like Doug Lorch, a retired IBM programmer whose year-two Brier score of 0.14 made him the best of thousands. The obvious objection: with enough coin-flippers, someone flips ten heads. Were these just the lucky tail of the distribution?

The data say no. If luck dominated, top performers should show heavy regression to the mean, tumbling back toward average the next year. Instead, year-to-year correlation in individual accuracy was about 0.65, and roughly 70% of superforecasters stayed in the top 2% the following year — against the ~2% you'd expect from pure chance. Regression existed but was modest and slow, meaning skill, not luck, drove most of the spread.

Two more facts seal it. First, when superforecasters were grouped into elite teams, their edge widened rather than evaporating — success didn't jinx them (team effects are chapter 7's subject). Second, the magnitude of the edge was striking: by Mellers's analysis, superforecasters looking 300 days ahead were more accurate than regular forecasters looking just 100 days ahead.

The strategic upshot frames the rest of the book. Foresight is real, measurable, and unevenly distributed — and the people who have it aren't celebrity pundits but disciplined thinkers using learnable methods. The chapters that follow unpack those methods: how superforecasters decompose questions (chapter 3), use fine-grained probabilities (chapter 4), update relentlessly (chapter 5), and treat themselves as works in perpetual beta (chapter 6).
In practice
Rewrite your next important prediction so it can be scored: a precisely defined event, a hard deadline, and a numeric probability — no "may," "could," or "significant."
Start a forecast log this week and compute your Brier score as outcomes resolve; judge yourself on many forecasts, never on one.
Check both halves of accuracy: track whether your 70% calls come true about 70% of the time (calibration), and whether you're hiding at 50% to avoid being wrong (resolution).
Always compare your score to a benchmark — chance, "no change" extrapolation, or a colleague answering the same questions — before congratulating yourself.
Audit your thinking style for hedgehog symptoms: if one Big Idea explains all your predictions and you rarely say "on the other hand," deliberately gather sources that cut against it.
Discount confidence and media fame when choosing whose forecasts to trust; ask instead for the person's track record on scored, time-bound predictions.
